content.text: Why Route Queries Instead of Indexing Everything Together

A production knowledge system rarely has a single data source. Documents live in a vector store, structured records in a SQL database, recent news on the web, and relationships in a graph. Trying to merge these into one index forces lossy transformations—converting SQL rows into text passages degrades precision queries, and embedding graph traversals loses structural information. Query routing instead keeps each source in its native format and dispatches each query to the source best suited for it. An intent classifier (typically a small LLM or embedding model) decides the route; each source then answers with its native query mechanism before the results are merged into a single generation call.

attrs.title: LLM Router classifies query intent and dispatches to vector store, SQL, web, or knowledge graph

content.text: The simplest router is a structured-output LLM call that maps a query to a data source label. Keeping the label set small and mutually exclusive (four or fewer categories) produces reliable routing without a dedicated fine-tuned classifier.

from typing import Literal
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)


class RouteDecision(BaseModel):
    datasource: Literal["vector_store", "sql_database", "web_search", "knowledge_graph"]
    reason: str = Field(description="One sentence explaining the routing choice")


router_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    (
        "system",
        """Route the user's question to the most appropriate data source.

vector_store   — conceptual or document questions (policies, manuals, articles)
sql_database   — queries about counts, aggregations, or specific record lookups
web_search     — current events, recent news, real-time information
knowledge_graph — relationship or path questions (who reports to whom, how X connects to Y)

Return only one datasource.""",
    ),
    ("human", "{question}"),
])

router = router_prompt | llm.with_structured_output(RouteDecision)


def classify_query(question: str) -> RouteDecision:
    return router.invoke({"question": question})


# Examples
print(classify_query("What is the refund policy?").datasource)       # vector_store
print(classify_query("How many orders were placed last month?").datasource)  # sql_database
print(classify_query("Who is the current CEO of Anthropic?").datasource)     # web_search

For high-throughput applications where routing latency matters, embed the query and compare it against a small set of representative examples per datasource. Cosine similarity against these anchors determines the route without any LLM call, cutting routing latency to under 10 ms.
LangChain's RunnableBranch wires the router output directly to the appropriate retrieval chain. Each branch is a (condition, chain) pair; the router decision selects which branch runs.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableBranch, RunnableLambda, RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_community.tools.tavily_search import TavilySearchResults

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
answer_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Answer using only this context."),
    ("human", "Context: {context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"),
])
answer_chain = answer_prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()


def make_context(docs_or_str) -> str:
    if isinstance(docs_or_str, str):
        return docs_or_str
    return "\n\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs_or_str)


# Chains for each datasource (stubs — replace with real retrievers / DB connectors)
vector_chain = RunnableLambda(lambda x: retriever.invoke(x["question"])) | RunnableLambda(make_context)
sql_chain    = RunnableLambda(lambda x: run_sql_query(x["question"]))
web_chain    = TavilySearchResults(max_results=3) | RunnableLambda(
    lambda r: "\n".join(item["content"] for item in r)
)

branch = RunnableBranch(
    (lambda x: x["datasource"] == "sql_database",    sql_chain),
    (lambda x: x["datasource"] == "web_search",      web_chain),
    vector_chain,  # default
)

full_chain = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(datasource=RunnableLambda(lambda x: classify_query(x["question"]).datasource))
    | RunnablePassthrough.assign(context=branch)
    | answer_chain
)

answer = full_chain.invoke({"question": "How many orders were placed last month?"})
print(answer)
RunnableBranch is concise but opaque—it's hard to inspect which branch ran in production. LangGraph makes each route a named node, so traces show exactly which datasource handled each query.
Choose the routing strategy based on query throughput and acceptable latency.

No classifier is perfect. Queries at category boundaries—like 'How many articles mention our refund policy?'—span both vector and SQL routes. A fallback chain handles misclassified or ambiguous queries without surfacing errors to users.

Build a labeled routing evaluation set to measure how often the classifier picks the correct datasource before deploying to production. Misrouting to SQL for a conceptual question produces a broken query; misrouting to vector for a count question produces a vague answer. Both failures are invisible without explicit measurement.
Query routing dispatches each query to the most appropriate data source (vector, SQL, web, or graph) rather than forcing all data into one index.
LLM structured-output classifiers give the highest accuracy; embedding cosine similarity is faster with acceptable accuracy for clear category boundaries.
RunnableBranch wires routing to retrieval chains concisely; LangGraph provides named nodes for better production observability.
Always add a fallback chain for empty results or misclassified queries to avoid silent failures.
Measure router accuracy with a labeled eval set before deployment — misrouting is invisible without explicit measurement.
